actions.thought: I hold pocket Queens (Qs Qd), which is a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game. My position is on the button, allowing me to take the initiative. Since I need to call the big blind of 100 to enter the pot, a raise is the optimal move to build the pot and potentially isolate one of the blinds. Opening to 300 chips (3x the big blind) is a standard and effective sizing to exert pressure and extract value.

KJo is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position facing a 3x open. Without reads on the Button's opening frequency, folding is the most solid and lowest variance approach early in the tournament, avoiding reverse implied odds.

93o is an extremely weak hand to defend from the Big Blind against a 3x Button raise. It lacks connectivity and high-card strength, and playing it out of position would likely lead to further losses. Folding is the best option to preserve chips for better spots.

5c 8d is an incredibly weak and disconnected hand. Even though I am on the button (first to act in 3-handed play), this hand lacks the equity or playability to justify opening, especially against two opponents. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Pro folded on the button, so the action is on me in the Small Blind. Ten-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the Big Blind and take the initiative in the hand.

KQs is a premium hand and a strong favorite against a standard small blind opening range. I am currently out of position, but 3-betting here allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, or set up a favorable post-flop scenario. A raise to 900 is a standard sizing to build the pot while still maintaining a reasonable stack-to-pot ratio.

I opened from the Small Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit, but the Big Blind (Flashlight) responded with a 3-bet to 900. T9o is too weak to call a 3-bet out of position, and 4-betting is unnecessarily aggressive at this stage. Folding is the disciplined play to avoid a potentially large loss with a marginal hand.

I am on the button with Ten-Five offsuit. In a three-handed game, while opening ranges are wider, T5o is still a very weak hand and does not meet the requirements for a standard open-raise from the button, especially with players behind who have shown aggression in previous hands. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Ah-7s, a solid hand in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind after the Button folded. I have the initiative advantage here. Raising to 300 is a standard play to pressure the Big Blind, build a pot with a playable hand, and potentially take down the blinds immediately. This allows me to control the action pre-flop.

We have 4c 6c, a suited one-gapper, in the Big Blind. The Small Blind has opened to 3x (300). While we would have position post-flop, 64s is slightly too weak to defend against a 3bb open, especially early in the tournament where preserving our stack and avoiding marginal spots is key. Folding is the safest and most standard play.

I am in the button position in a 3-handed game. With King-Seven offsuit, I have a reasonable hand to attempt an open-raise to steal the blinds. Raising from the button is a standard aggressive play in short-handed poker to accumulate chips before the flop.
K6o is too weak of a hand to play out of position from the small blind against a button raise. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am holding Pocket Queens (Qs Qh), which is a premium starting hand. Flashlight raised to 250 from the button, a position from which players typically open with a wide range. Pro has folded the Small Blind. I should 3-bet (re-raise) for value and to protect my hand. A raise to 800 (3.2x the initial raise) is a standard size to build the pot while out of position.

Opening with King-Seven offsuit is a standard steal attempt on the button in 3-handed play. However, facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind, my hand lacks sufficient equity to continue. I am likely dominated by their 3-betting range and will have difficulty navigating post-flop play. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision.

I am on the Button 3-handed with 76s. This is a solid suited connector that plays very well in position. An open raise is standard here to attempt to steal the blinds or play a post-flop pot in position with an advantage. A raise size of 2.5x (250) gives me a good price on a steal while building a pot for when I flop well.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. The Button has opened with a raise to 250. This is a very weak hand to play out of position, and calling or raising would put me at a significant disadvantage post-flop against a likely wider but stronger range from the Button. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I am in the big blind with 5s-2c, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite the decent pot odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 550), playing such a low-equity hand out of position against an opponent's opening range is strategically unsound. It is better to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation rather than engaging with a dominated hand.

I am on the button with 92o, which is a very weak starting hand. While a standard strategy involves opening a wide range from the button in a 3-handed game, 92o is significantly below the threshold for a profitable open. Folding is the most standard and lowest-risk play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

With Ace-King suited in the small blind after the button folds, this is a premium hand that I should raise to take the initiative against the big blind. A standard open raise size of 3x the big blind is appropriate here to build value and potentially take down the pot pre-flop.

KTo is a solid holding against an active Small Blind opening range. Since I have position post-flop, calling is standard and profitable. It keeps the opponent's weaker hands in the pot and allows me to realize my equity without over-committing pre-flop.

I have flopped top pair, top kicker (Ace-King on King-Ten-Nine rainbow). While the board has some straight possibilities (J-Q, J-8, Q-J), my hand is very strong here. I should lead out for value and to charge draws. Betting 350 into a 600 pot is a standard sizing to protect against equity from drawing hands while continuing to build the pot with my top pair.

I flopped top two pair on a highly coordinated and draw-heavy board (9s Kc Td). The SB c-bets 350. I need to raise for value against hands like AK, KQ, KJ and to charge the many straight draws (QJ, AQ, AJ, J8) that have good equity against me. Raising to 1100 puts pressure on these draws.

On a rainbow K-T-9 board, my top pair with an Ace kicker is too strong to fold to a single raise, even if the opponent has some potential straight draws or two-pair combinations in their range. The raise to 1100 could easily be a semi-bluff with a draw like Q-J or an overplayed middle pair. Calling allows me to keep the pot size manageable and evaluate the turn, while avoiding committing my stack to a potential set or straight that beats me.

The turn brings an Ace, giving me top two pair (Aces and Kings). While this is a very strong hand, the opponent's aggressive flop raise indicates they likely have a set (99, TT), two pair (KT, K9), or a straight (QJ). Betting into that range might be value-cutting myself if I am already behind a straight, or pushing out hands I beat if I bet too large. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot, keep their bluffs in, and re-evaluate their action on the river.

The Ace on the turn is a very dangerous card for my hand. It completes the QJ straight and improves hands like AK and AT to a better two pair than my Kings and Tens. Since the SB called a flop raise on a wet board, their range is heavily weighted towards these exact hands, along with some draws like AQ/AJ. Betting here risks isolating myself against better hands, as worse hands like KQ or K9 might fold, and draws might check-raise. Checking behind controls the pot size, realizes my equity, and allows me to evaluate the river or catch bluffs from missed straight draws.

On the river with a final board of 9s-Kc-Td-Ah-4s, I hold two pair (Aces and Kings). The board contains possibilities for a straight (Q-J), which would have been completed on the turn. Given that the opponent raised the flop and then checked the turn, their range is polarized between a monster hand (sets like 99, TT, or a straight) and a cautious hand that they are trying to get to showdown cheaply. Betting into this range is risky because I will likely only be called by better hands (like a straight or sets) and fold out hands that I currently beat (like missed draws or one-pair hands). Checking is the most balanced approach to realize my showdown value and control the pot size without bloating it when I might be behind.

The river 4s is a relative blank. My opponent checked again after I checked back the turn. The board is very scary, with the Ace on the turn completing straight draws like QJ and improving hands like AK, AT, or AQ. If I bet here, it is very thin for value: worse hands like KQ or KJ are highly likely to fold on this board after my flop raise, while better hands like AK, AT, A9, or trapped straights will snap-call or check-raise. Checking behind allows me to safely get to showdown with my two pair and beat the missed draws or weaker Kings.

I am dealt 4-2 offsuit, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While the button offers positional advantage, this hand lacks the equity to contest a pot profitably against the blinds. Folding here is the standard, disciplined approach to protect my stack early in the tournament.

With AJ offsuit in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a strong hand for a blind versus blind encounter. Raising to 300 applies pressure on the Big Blind, denies equity to weaker hands, and builds the pot with what is likely the best hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 2c, which is a very weak hand. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Although I am getting 3:1 odds on a call, 6-2 offsuit performs poorly post-flop and I would be playing out of position. Standard professional strategy dictates a fold here to preserve chips for better opportunities.

K3o is a very marginal and weak hand to open from the button, even three-handed. The playability post-flop is poor, and if we face a 3-bet or get called, we are often dominated by better Kings or medium/strong hands in the blinds' ranges. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve the stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Q5o. The Button (Pro) has folded. This leaves me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). Q5o is a weak hand to play out of position, especially against Flashlight who has shown significant aggression in previous hands (raising and 3-betting frequently). While I could complete or raise to steal, I prefer to fold this weak hand and wait for a more advantageous situation, avoiding unnecessary conflict with the chip leader while being out of position.

T6o is a weak hand even in a 3-handed game on the button. While the button warrants a wide opening range, T6o is generally below the threshold for a standard raise against active opponents like Flashlight and Pro. Given the early stage of the tournament and the relative stack sizes, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With the Button having folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. King-Eight suited is a strong enough hand to warrant a raise from the small blind to seize the initiative and potentially win the blinds. A raise to 250 (2.5x the big blind) is a standard sizing to apply pressure.

Facing a 2.5x open-raise from the small blind, my 75 offsuit is too weak to defend. It lacks both high card value and suitedness, making it highly unprofitable to play post-flop, even with position. The most disciplined and optimal action is to fold.

In a 3-handed game, the button is a position of significant power. Holding 5c9c, while not a premium hand, is strong enough to open for a raise to steal the blinds and leverage my positional advantage. With a healthy stack, I can afford to play aggressively in this spot.

Flashlight opened from the Button to 250. I have 97o in the Small Blind, which is a weak, unsuited two-gapper. Playing this hand out of position is unprofitable, so the standard and correct play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium hand in 3-handed play. Flashlight (the Button) has opened to 250. Standard play here is to 3-bet for value and to seize the initiative. A call would underrepresent my hand strength, but 3-betting helps define Flashlight's range and builds a pot. I will raise to 850, which is approximately 3.4 times the initial raise size.

Flash has 3-bet from the big blind against my button open. While I have position, 5c9c is a weak, speculative hand and does not perform well against a strong 3-betting range. Calling is mathematically unprofitable given the lack of implied odds and the risk of being forced to fold on most flops. I will fold and preserve my stack.

With A2o on the button in a 3-handed game, our hand is quite marginal. While a steal is possible, A2o plays poorly post-flop and can often be dominated if we are called or 3-bet by the blinds. Folding is a solid, disciplined decision to preserve our stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. The Button has folded, leaving just me and the Big Blind. Ten-Six offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position. While I could attempt a steal by raising, the Big Blind has shown aggression in previous hands. Folding is the safest and most efficient play here to avoid a potentially losing situation out of position with a marginal hand.

I am holding Pocket Tens on the button, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. My strategy is to raise to build the pot and take control of the hand from a position of strength. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to apply pressure on the blinds and isolate.

I am in the small blind with 9-8 offsuit, which is a weak hand to play out of position against a raise from the button. Given that I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand and the hand strength is marginal at best, folding is the most disciplined and +EV decision to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 9s, which is a weak offsuit connector. The button (Flash) has raised to 3x the big blind (300). Offsuit connectors like 98o play poorly out of position post-flop, and calling here would likely lead to bleeding chips over time. The standard and most profitable decision is to fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I want to build a pot and take the initiative against the small and big blinds. A standard raise to 300 is consistent with my opening strategy and allows me to see a flop with control of the hand.

We hold pocket sixes in the small blind facing a button open. This is a strong enough hand to see a flop and set-mine given our stack depth (over 80 BBs). While the Big Blind has shown some squeeze tendencies, the implied odds of hitting a set against the pre-flop raiser are favorable enough to flat call. If we face a large 3-bet from the BB, we can comfortably fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) raised to 300, and the Small Blind (Pro) called. The pot is currently 700, and it costs me 200 more to call. This gives me pot odds of 3.5:1. Given that I am closing the pre-flop action and my hand has decent playability in a multi-way pot, calling is the most standard and mathematically sound play to realize my equity.

We have a marginal made hand with pocket sixes (third pair) on a J-5-4 flop, facing two opponents. As the pre-flop caller out of position in a multi-way pot, the standard and safest approach is to check our entire range to the pre-flop raiser. This allows us to evaluate the action from the Button and the Big Blind before committing any more chips.

I have flopped Top Pair (Jacks) with a Ten kicker. This is a strong hand on a relatively dry board, though there are two diamonds and a small straight draw (4-5). Since the Small Blind has already checked and the Button was the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the Button to see if they continue with a bet. This allows me to control the size of the pot and potentially catch a bluff or a continuation bet from a wider range.

I am in position as the pre-flop aggressor. The board of four-diamonds, five-clubs, and jack-diamonds is relatively uncoordinated. With my Ace-Queen, I hold two overcards and a backdoor nut flush draw. Betting 450 into the 900 pot is a standard continuation bet to maintain my initiative and apply pressure to the blinds, who checked to me, potentially forcing folds from weaker holdings.

With pocket sixes on a Jd-5c-4d board, I have third pair with no diamond to provide backup equity. The pre-flop raiser (Flashlight) fired a half-pot continuation bet into two opponents, showing strength on a fairly wet and coordinated board. Furthermore, the Big Blind is still left to act behind me. Given the likelihood that I am currently behind a jack, a better pocket pair, or up against strong draws, and the looming threat of action from the Big Blind, folding is the safest and highest expected value decision.

I have top pair (Jacks) with a decent kicker on a board that isn't overly connected. The Button's bet of 450 into a 900 pot is a standard continuation bet that could be made with a wide range of hands including draws, smaller pairs, or total bluffs. With the Small Blind folding, I am heads-up. Folding top pair here is too tight, and raising would likely isolate me against only stronger hands like AJ, KJ, or sets. Calling is the most balanced approach, keeping the pot manageable and allowing me to evaluate the turn card.

The Queen of Diamonds on the turn is a very unfavorable card for my Jack-Ten. It is an overcard to my pair of Jacks and also completes a possible diamond flush. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop and flop aggressor (Flashlight), the most prudent play is to check. This allows me to control the pot size and see how the opponent reacts to the board texture change. If the opponent bets, I will have to carefully evaluate whether my second pair still has enough equity to continue, given the increased likelihood that they have a Queen, a flush, or a better hand.

On the turn, the Queen of Diamonds improves my hand to top pair and gives me the nut flush draw. The board is getting wetter with three diamonds now, but my holdings are very strong. Flash checked to me, which is a sign of weakness or pot control. I have significant equity and value, so betting is the best course of action to build the pot against potential draws or weaker pairs that may have called the flop. A bet of 900 into the 1800 pot is a solid sizing to maximize value and charge draws while maintaining control.

The Queen of Diamonds is a highly unfavorable card for my hand. It is an overcard to my pair of Jacks and completes the diamond flush. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up their flop continuation bet with a larger bet on the turn, suggesting significant strength or a well-timed semi-bluff. My hand, which was top pair on the flop, has now been demoted to second pair on a board where several drawing hands have completed. Given the board texture and the betting action, I am likely behind a Queen, a flush, or a set. Calling here would put me in a difficult position on the river, especially out of position. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.
I am on the Button with 6s 3h, which is an extremely weak, unplayable hand. I will fold and save my chips for better opportunities.

96o is a weak starting hand. Even in a 3-handed game when the Button folds, completing or raising from the Small Blind with 9-high offsuit is marginal. Given Flashlight's (BB) aggressive history in previous hands, I prefer to fold and wait for a better opportunity rather than play a weak hand out of position against the chip leader.

I am on the button with Qd 4d. In a 3-handed game, this hand is strong enough to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down immediately. Standard button opening ranges usually include most suited Queens. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind.

Jack-Eight offsuit is a weak hand, particularly out of position from the Small Blind against an open from the button. Calling or re-raising would bloat the pot in a disadvantageous position with a dominated hand. With 135 hands remaining, preserving my stack for better equity spots is the prudent strategy.

Ten-Two offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, no straight or flush potential, and is easily dominated. Even getting okay pot odds from the Big Blind against a Button raise, folding is the standard and correct decision.

I am on the button with Q-T offsuit, which is a solid hand to open in a 3-handed game. I want to continue my aggressive playstyle and put pressure on the blinds to potentially take down the pot pre-flop or secure position. A raise to 300 is consistent with my previous opening sizing.
Facing a 3x open from the button, King-Three suited in the small blind is too weak to call or 3-bet profitably. Folding is the standard play to avoid playing out of position with a marginal holding.

I am in the Big Blind with Q5o. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for 300. While the pot odds are approximately 2.25:1, Q5o is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position against the chip leader. In a 3-handed game, ranges are wider, but defending with this hand is likely to lead to -EV situations on later streets. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Holding suited connectors (5c4c) on the Button gives me a great opportunity to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I'll raise to 2.5x to take the initiative, knowing I can comfortably fold to a 3-bet or play well post-flop if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 78o. The Button (Pro) has opened for 2.5x. While 78o has some connectivity, it is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raiser. Furthermore, the Big Blind (Flashlight) is still to act and could squeeze. Given the tournament context and the difficulty of playing this hand post-flop without initiative, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

I am in the big blind with Kh9s, facing a button open of 250 from Pro. The small blind has folded. Given the pot odds and my position, calling is a standard, low-variance play to see a flop. I have decent equity against a button opening range and it is better to defend rather than fold here.

I am in the big blind with King-high and a backdoor heart draw on an Ace-high board. The Ace on the board is highly likely to have connected with the pre-flop aggressor's (Pro) range. As I have no pair and no strong draw, checking is the standard play to control the pot and evaluate the opponent's action. Betting here would be an unnecessary bluff into a range that significantly out-positions and out-ranges me.

As the pre-flop raiser, this Ace-high board strongly favors my range. I have a gutshot straight draw to the wheel, providing a bit of backdoor equity. A standard continuation bet of around 1/3 to 1/2 pot should be enough to fold out Flashlight's overcards, small pairs, and weaker holdings.

The board is Ace-high, which heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's (Pro) range. With only King-high and no made pair, I have essentially zero showdown value and limited equity even with the backdoor flush draw. While the pot odds are decent, playing out of position against an opponent who likely has an Ace or better makes calling a losing proposition in the long run. Folding is the most disciplined and effective way to protect my stack.

I am on the button with King-Queen of spades, which is a premium hand in a three-handed game. Raising here is standard to take control of the hand and build the pot with a strong range advantage. A 3x big blind raise is a standard and effective sizing in this position.

I am in the small blind holding 3c9d, which is an extremely weak, unsuited hand with no playability. Calling is out of the question as I would be playing out of position with a dominated range against the button. Folding is the only disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 3bb raise from the Button. My hand, 84s, is very weak, disconnected, and easily dominated. Playing this hand out of position against a preflop raiser is not profitable, even with suited cards, so folding is the standard and correct decision.

K-5 offsuit is a weak, unsuited hand that does not warrant an open or a call from the button. It lacks sufficient showdown value and is dominated by many cards in my opponents' ranges. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for better equity spots.

It folds to me in the Small Blind and I hold A6o. This is a solid hand to try and steal the big blind. I will raise to 3x to apply pressure and ideally take down the pot uncontested, avoiding having to play post-flop out of position with a marginal kicker.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8d 6h. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I only need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 600, giving me 3:1 odds, 8-6 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position. Against a player labeled as 'Pro' who has shown aggression, calling here often leads to difficult post-flop situations where I will struggle to realize my equity. In Hand #7, a similar situation occurred and I folded. Given the 150-hand tournament structure, I prefer to wait for better spots rather than defending with marginal hands that perform poorly OOP.

9h8h is a strong suited connector, making it a standard open from the Button in a 3-handed game. I'll raise to 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) to take the initiative, potentially steal the blinds, or build a pot in position with a hand that plays very well post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Five offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened for 2.5x the big blind. T5o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stack depths and the hand strength, there is no reason to defend this hand or attempt a bluff 3-bet. Folding is the most profitable decision in the long run.

5-2 offsuit is a bottom-tier hand with extremely low equity. Even though the pot odds are tempting, playing this hand out of position against the button is a losing proposition in the long run. Folding is the most disciplined and correct strategic choice.

In a 3-handed game, the button should open a wide range. My hand, 6h 5c, is a bit weak but acceptable for a steal attempt. More importantly, observing the previous hands, 'Pro' has folded premium hands like QQ and AK to small pre-flop raises. This suggests extremely high fold equity. I will raise to 300 to pressure the blinds.

King-four offsuit is a weak hand with no playability, especially from the small blind position out of position. Given the aggressive pre-flop action from the button, calling would be negative expectation. It is best to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

6s 7s is a premium suited connector that plays very well post-flop. Facing a standard 3x open from the Button, we are getting favorable pot odds to defend our Big Blind. Calling to see a flop is a standard, profitable play.

I have a strong flush draw on the flop with 6s 7s on a 3s 2c 8s board. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard and most balanced play is to check. This allows the pre-flop aggressor to act and gives me the option to check-raise or call depending on their sizing.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the board (3s 2c 8s) is relatively dry, likely missing most of the Big Blind's defending range. I have a gutshot straight draw with my 6h 5c. Given that 'Pro' has shown a tendency to play very conservatively in previous hands (folding hands like QQ and AK pre-flop), a continuation bet here has high fold equity. I will bet 400 into a 650 pot to take control and ideally end the hand now.

I have a flush draw with 6s 7s on a 3s 2c 8s board. Facing a continuation bet of 400 from the Button into a 650 pot, I am getting roughly 2.6-to-1 on a call, needing about 27.5% equity. With 9 outs to a flush, I have around 35% equity to hit by the river, making this a profitable call. While check-raising as a semi-bluff is an option, playing aggressively with a medium-high flush draw out of position can be risky if my opponent holds a higher spade draw or a strong made hand. Calling allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn card (4c) adds a gutshot straight draw to my existing flush draw, giving me around 12 outs to improve on the river. Being out of position against the flop aggressor, the standard play is to check. This maintains pot control, allows my opponent to continue bluffing or value betting, and gives me the opportunity to evaluate their turn bet sizing before deciding whether to call or check-raise as a semi-bluff.

I have turned the nut straight (2-3-4-5-6) with my 6-5. The 4 on the turn completed the draw I was semi-bluffing on the flop. Since Pro has called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, they likely have a made hand like top pair (8x), a pocket pair, or perhaps a lower straight draw (A5). Pro has checked the turn, so I will bet 800 into the 1450 pot to extract value. This sizing is large enough to build the pot but hopefully not so large that it forces a fold from a conservative player with an 8.

Facing an 800 bet into a 1450 pot on the turn, I am getting roughly 2.8-to-1 pot odds, needing about 26% direct equity to profitably call. With my flush draw and a gutshot straight draw to an 8-high straight (any 5 or any spade), I have 12 outs to improve my hand. This gives me approximately 26% equity to hit on the river, perfectly matching the required odds. When we factor in the implied odds of extracting more chips from our opponent if we do hit our draw on the river, calling becomes the optimal and most profitable choice.

I missed both my flush draw and straight draw on the river, ending up with just 7-high. Being first to act out of position, the standard play is to check. Leading out as a bluff after check-calling two streets is often unconvincing and can easily be called by the opponent's value range that double-barreled the flop and turn. I will check and plan to fold if my opponent bets.

I have a straight (2-3-4-5-6), which was the nut straight on the turn. The river King is a blank for my hand. Pro has played conservatively throughout the tournament but has called three streets in this hand, suggesting they have a made hand like top pair or a pocket pair. Since Pro checked the river, I will place a value bet of 1600, which is slightly over half the pot. This sizing is intended to extract maximum value from a hand like an 8, a pocket pair, or a King that might have improved on the river, without being so large as to force a fold from a cautious player.

I completely missed my flush and straight draws, ending up with just 7-high on the river. My opponent has fired a substantial third barrel of 1600. With absolutely zero showdown value, calling is impossible. Attempting a massive check-raise bluff here is highly risky and unconvincing given my passive line on the previous streets, and it would unnecessarily jeopardize a large portion of my stack. The only logical and optimal play is to fold.

7d 4c is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially in a three-handed game. There is no strategic value in playing this hand from the button. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak hand (9d2s). Flashlight folded on the button. Since my hand has very little playability and I would be out of position if called, folding is the most profitable decision here rather than attempting to steal or limp.
AJo is a strong starting hand, especially from the Button. I will make a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind to either pick up the blinds directly or play a pot in position with a strong holding.

King-Queen offsuit is a premium hand in 3-handed play. Since Pro raised from the button, they likely have a wide range. I will 3-bet (raise) to 800 to take the initiative, build a value pot, and potentially force a fold from the button or the big blind. Calling out of position is less ideal than asserting strength pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 4d, which is an extremely weak hand. Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind after an open from the Button indicates a strong range from at least one opponent. There is no strategic justification for calling with such a poor holding out of position. Folding is the only correct decision.

By betting now, I can extract value from weaker made hands like a Jack or pocket pairs, as well as charge drawing hands a premium to see the turn. Pocket Jacks are a strong premium pair in this position. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing to accomplish this while maintaining control of the hand.

Holding 5d 8d in the Small Blind facing a Button open to 3bb. This is a speculative suited gapper that plays poorly out of position, especially with the Big Blind still left to act behind me. Squeezing or calling are both unprofitable over the long run in this spot, so folding is the standard and safest play to preserve my stack.

I am holding a small pocket pair (2h 2c) in the Big Blind. The button (Flashlight) has raised to 300. With the small blind folding, it is 200 more for me to call into a pot of 450, giving me 2.25:1 odds. This is a standard call to set-mine or see a favorable flop, as folding a pocket pair to a single raise from the button is too conservative, and raising out of position with the smallest pair is unnecessarily risky.

I have bottom pair (22) on a very coordinated board of Q-A-A. This board heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's (Flashlight) range, which includes many Aces, Queens, and higher pocket pairs. There is no reason to lead out here as a bluff or for value. I will check and likely fold to any significant aggression, as my hand has very little potential to improve or win at showdown.

The flop of Ace-Ace-Queen is extremely coordinated and hits the Big Blind's calling range very hard, as they will have many Aces and Queens in their flatting range. Betting would likely only be called by better hands or hands that have me crushed, and I would be folding out nothing that I currently beat. Checking back is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and realize equity, allowing me to re-evaluate on the turn while keeping the pot manageable against a potential trap.

I am holding a pair of deuces on a board of Q-A-A-K. While I technically have two pair (Aces and Deuces), the board contains three cards higher than my pair, meaning any opponent holding a card higher than a deuce has a better two-pair or better. There is no merit in betting here as a bluff or for value; checking and hoping to get to a cheap showdown or folding to any aggression is the optimal play.

The board is extremely dangerous with A-A-K-Q, meaning any Ten makes a straight, and any Ace, King, or Queen has me beaten. My pocket Jacks have effectively zero showdown value against any hand that calls a bet. Checking behind is the only logical play here to keep the pot small and potentially reach a cheap showdown if the opponent is also weak, as I cannot extract value from any worse hand.

The river card is a Six, making the board Q-A-A-K-6. My pocket deuces are now completely 'counterfeited,' meaning the five cards on the board (Aces, Queen, King, and Six) are higher than my pair. By checking, I am looking to reach a showdown. In most scenarios where Flashlight hasn't improved, we will simply split the pot by 'playing the board.' There is no value in betting, as I cannot represent a hand strong enough to make a better hand fold, nor will a worse hand call.

The board shows Qs-Ah-Ac-Ks-6c. My pocket Jacks are effectively a bluff catcher. The opponent checked the river, indicating they likely have a weak hand, a missed draw, or a small pair. Betting provides no value, as I will only be called by better hands (any Ace, King, Queen, or Ten that made a straight) and will fold out worse hands. Checking behind is the optimal play to realize my showdown equity.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (6d 4d). The button has opened, and I am out of position. Calling here would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with a low-equity hand. It is standard discipline to fold this hand and preserve my stack.
